1: Do not envy evil people or wish to be in their company — the prohibition against coveting the wicked's apparent advantage. The NT's 'do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewal of your mind' (Romans 12:2) addresses the same pull. Psalm 73 is the extended wisdom-meditation on this temptation: the poet nearly lost his footing until he entered the sanctuary and saw the end of the wicked. Christ is the sanctuary where the perspective is restored; in his light, the company of the wicked offers nothing worth envying.
2: For their heart plans violence, and their lips talk about causing trouble — the inner life of the wicked exposed. Jesus's forensic teaching: 'Out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false witness, slander' (Matthew 15:19). The wicked whose hearts plan violence are what Christ diagnoses as the human condition without transformation. The new heart given in the new covenant (Ezekiel 36:26-27) replaces the heart that plans violence with the heart that loves; Christ is the mediator of that transformation.
3: By wisdom a house is built, and by understanding it is established — the parallel to Proverbs 3:19-20 (by wisdom the LORD founded the earth) applied to human construction. Jesus builds on this in Matthew 7:24-27: the wise builder who hears his words and does them builds on rock; the foolish one builds on sand. 1 Corinthians 3:10-11 makes the christological foundation explicit: 'no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ.' Wisdom builds the house; Christ is the foundation the house is built on.
4: Through knowledge the rooms are filled with all kinds of precious and pleasant treasures — the interior abundance of the wisdom-built house. The NT: 'in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell' (Colossians 1:19); 'the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily' in Christ (Colossians 2:9). The house filled with precious treasures through knowledge is a type of the fullness that dwells in Christ — and by extension, the fullness given to those who are 'in him who is the head of all rule and authority' (Colossians 2:10). The knowledge that fills the rooms is ultimately the knowledge of Christ.
5: A wise man is strong, and a man of knowledge grows in might — wisdom as true power. The NT's paradox: 'the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God' (1 Corinthians 1:18). The strength that wisdom gives is not the strength of armies but the strength of God: 'not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says the LORD of hosts' (Zechariah 4:6). Christ is 'the power of God and the wisdom of God' (1 Corinthians 1:24) — the one in whom true might and true wisdom are identical.
6: For by wise guidance you can wage war, and victory depends on having many advisers — the wisdom of counsel in conflict. The NT: 'the weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh but have divine power to destroy strongholds' (2 Corinthians 10:4). The wise guidance Proverbs commends is the Spirit who leads into all truth (John 16:13): 'the Counselor... will teach you all things' (John 14:26). Victory in spiritual warfare depends on the counsel of the Spirit of Christ, the many-counseled wisdom of God disclosed in the one who bore all the names of Isaiah 9:6: Wonderful Counselor.
7: Wisdom is beyond a fool's reach; he has nothing to say at the city gate — the inaccessibility of wisdom to the spiritually unformed. The NT parallel: 'The natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit of God, for they are folly to him, and he is not able to understand them because they are spiritually discerned' (1 Corinthians 2:14). The wisdom that the fool cannot reach is precisely what Paul declares hidden in Christ: 'wisdom among the mature, a secret and hidden wisdom of God' (1 Corinthians 2:6-7) — accessible only to those who have the Spirit who searches even the depths of God.
8: Whoever schemes to do evil will be known as a troublemaker — the social exposure of the schemer. The hypocrisy that schemes while appearing upright is what Jesus confronts in the religious leaders: 'Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites!' (Matthew 23:27). The troublemaker who is finally exposed mirrors the judgment of the last day: 'nothing is covered up that will not be revealed' (Luke 12:2). Christ both exposes evil schemes in his earthly ministry and will fully reveal them at the judgment (1 Corinthians 4:5: the Lord will bring to light the things now hidden in darkness).
9: The intention behind foolish schemes is sin, and the mocker is detestable to everyone — sin beginning in intention, not merely in action. Jesus extends this exactly: You have heard it was said, you shall not commit adultery, but I say to you that everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery in his heart (Matthew 5:27-28). The wisdom principle that intention is already sin is radicalized by Christ, who traces sin to its root in the heart and calls for transformation at that level — not merely behavioral control but new birth.
10: If you falter in times of trouble, how small is your strength! — the test of trouble as the measure of wisdom's depth. Hebrews 12:1-3 directs this endurance precisely to Christ: 'let us run with endurance the race that is set before us, looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross.' The strength that does not falter in trouble is not stoic resilience but Christ-anchored faith; the one who endured the cross gives endurance to those who look to him.
11: Rescue those being taken away to death; hold back those stumbling toward slaughter — the active advocacy for the condemned. James's 'save a soul from death' (James 5:20) and the parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37) engage this call. Christ himself embodies it at the supreme level: he came to rescue those 'being taken away to death' — enslaved to sin, destined for condemnation — and his cross is the rescue operation. The call to intervene for the condemned is a call to participate in the rescue pattern that defines Christ's own mission.
12: If you say, 'We did not know this,' does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? — the impossibility of pleading ignorance before the God who knows. Hebrews 4:13: 'No creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account.' Jesus: 'I tell you, on the day of judgment people will give account for every careless word they speak' (Matthew 12:36). The God who weighs hearts and repays according to deeds (Proverbs 24:12b) is the God whose omniscience is disclosed fully in Christ, who 'knew what was in man' (John 2:25).
13: My son, eat honey because it is good, and the honeycomb that is sweet to your taste — wisdom tasted as pleasure. Psalms 19:10 uses the same image: God's law is 'sweeter also than honey and drippings of the honeycomb.' Psalms 119:103: 'How sweet are your words to my taste, sweeter than honey to my mouth!' The sweetness of wisdom's word is a theme that culminates in Revelation 10:9-10 where John eats the scroll and it is sweet in the mouth but bitter in the stomach — wisdom received is sweet; wisdom enacted in judgment is bitter. Christ is the word that is both sweet and costly.
14: So is wisdom to your soul; when you find it, there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off — wisdom as the ground of eschatological hope. The NT's hope is grounded in a person: 'Christ in you, the hope of glory' (Colossians 1:27). Finding wisdom means finding Christ — and finding Christ means the future is secured. 'We have this as a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul, a hope that enters into the inner place behind the curtain, where Jesus has gone as a forerunner on our behalf' (Hebrews 6:19-20). The uncut hope of Proverbs 24:14 is the anchor of Hebrews 6.
15: Do not lie in wait against the home of the righteous; do not raid his dwelling place — the prohibition against targeting the righteous. The wicked who target the righteous are the structural opponents of Christ's people: 'If the world hates you, know that it has hated me before it hated you' (John 15:18). Christ himself was the righteous one targeted by ambush — the councils and plots against him (Matthew 26:3-5). The prohibition in Proverbs is violated against Christ; its violation becomes the occasion for the resurrection that vindicates the righteous sufferer.
16: For a righteous person falls seven times and gets up again, but the wicked are brought down by calamity — the resilience of the righteous through repeated falls. Seven falls and seven risings is the wisdom way of saying: the righteous are never finally defeated. The NT: 'We are afflicted in every way, but not crushed; perplexed, but not driven to despair; persecuted, but not forsaken; struck down, but not destroyed' (2 Corinthians 4:8-9). Christ's own fall into death is the ultimate instance — and his rising the ultimate getting up again. The pattern of the righteous person who falls and rises is the shape of Easter applied to all who are in Christ.
17: Do not gloat when your enemy falls, and do not let your heart be glad when he stumbles — the restraint of enemy-love, even in victory. Jesus makes this explicit: 'Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you' (Matthew 5:44). The restraint Proverbs calls for becomes in Christ the positive command to active love of enemies — not merely not gloating but pursuing their good. Christ embodies this from the cross: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do' (Luke 23:34). No gloating in victory; only love for those who stumble.
18: Lest the LORD see it and be displeased, and he turn his anger away from your enemy — the surprising logic: gloating over an enemy's downfall can move God to spare the enemy. This is the hidden mercy in God's justice: his displeasure at cruelty can become the basis for the adversary's rescue. The NT extends this: 'Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord' (Romans 12:19) — the removal of vengeance from human hands is itself a form of grace toward the enemy. God's justice is trustworthy; human gloating is not, and it can become a reversal of God's purposes.
19: Do not fret because of evildoers or be envious of the wicked — the peace of those who have eschatological perspective. Psalm 37 extends this over 40 verses: the prosperity of the wicked is temporary; the inheritance of the righteous is permanent. The NT's transformation: 'Do not be anxious about anything, but in everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God. And the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus' (Philippians 4:6-7). The peace that prevents fretting is the peace of Christ.
20: For the evil person has no future reward; the lamp of the wicked will be snuffed out — the eschatological finality of the wicked's end. Jesus uses the same lamp imagery: 'the foolish virgins' lamps went out (Matthew 25:8); 'there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth' (Matthew 25:30). The lamp snuffed out is the definitive end; the lamp that burns is the life of the faithful disciple (Matthew 5:16: let your light shine before others). Christ is 'the light of the world' (John 8:12) — the one whose lamp cannot be snuffed out, the source from which all true lamps are lit.
21: My son, fear the LORD and the king, and do not associate with those who seek to change — the call to proper submission to authority. The NT: 'Be subject for the Lord's sake to every human institution, whether it be to the emperor as supreme or to governors as sent by him' (1 Peter 2:13-14); 'Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God' (Romans 13:1). Christ himself submitted to Roman authority even in his condemnation (John 19:11: you would have no authority over me at all unless it had been given you from above), modeling the submission he calls his people toward.
22: For their disaster will come suddenly, and who knows what destruction may come from them both? — the unpredictable judgment that falls on the rebellious. The sudden disaster is a consistent eschatological warning: while people are saying peace and security, sudden destruction will come upon them (1 Thessalonians 5:3). The warning to avoid those who seek to change — to resist established order — is the wisdom observation that revolutionary destabilization reaps its own judgment. The kingdom of Christ does not come through violent overthrow but through the patient faithfulness that submitted even to the cross.
23: These also are sayings of the wise: showing partiality in judgment is not right — the opening of a supplementary wisdom collection. The impartiality of God as judge is a cornerstone of both OT and NT theology: 'God shows no partiality' (Acts 10:34; Romans 2:11; 1 Peter 1:17). Christ himself demonstrated this impartiality — eating with Pharisees and sinners alike, healing both Jews and Gentiles, dying for the ungodly of every tribe and tongue. The judge who shows no partiality is the same God who gives 'every good gift' impartially (James 1:17).
24: Whoever tells the guilty, 'You are innocent,' will be cursed by peoples and condemned by nations — the cost of false acquittal. Pilate is the supreme historical instance: he declared Jesus innocent (John 18:38: I find no guilt in him) and then condemned him anyway — the double falseness of calling the innocent guilty in deed while calling him innocent in word. The perversion of justice that Proverbs condemns is carried to its extreme in the trial of Christ, where the truly innocent was condemned and the guilty were released (Barabbas, Mark 15:6-15). The judgment of nations against false judges is ultimately the judgment at which Christ is vindicated.
25: But those who rebuke the wicked will find delight, and a generous blessing will come upon them — the vindication of the honest rebuke. Nathan's rebuke of David (2 Samuel 12:1-7) is the OT model; John the Baptist's rebuke of Herod (Mark 6:18) and Jesus's rebukes of the religious leaders (Matt 23) are the NT expressions. The blessing on honest rebukers comes at cost — John was beheaded; Jesus was crucified — but the delight and blessing that Proverbs promises are eschatological: 'Blessed are you when others revile you and persecute you and utter all kinds of evil against you falsely on my account. Rejoice and be glad, for your reward is great in heaven' (Matthew 5:11-12).
26: Whoever gives an honest answer is like a kiss on the lips — truthful speech as intimate gift. The NT: 'Speaking the truth in love, we are to grow up in every way into him who is the head, into Christ' (Ephesians 4:15). The honest answer that is 'like a kiss' combines intimacy and truth — the combination that Christ embodies: 'full of grace and truth' (John 1:14). The kiss of Judas was the inversion — intimacy used as betrayal. Christ's truth-speaking was always also an act of love toward the hearer, even when confrontational.
27: Prepare your outdoor work and get your fields ready; after that, build your house — the right ordering of priorities. Jesus uses counting-the-cost language that parallels this: 'Which of you, desiring to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost?' (Luke 14:28). The ordering of outdoor work before household building is the wisdom that puts foundation before superstructure. Christ calls his disciples to count the cost of discipleship before committing — not to discourage but to ensure the house is built on the right foundation in the right order.
28: Do not testify against your neighbor without cause; do not deceive with your lips — the covenant prohibition of false witness (the ninth commandment). Jesus addresses false witness in his trial (Matthew 26:59-60: the chief priests sought false testimony), and his own example — 'When he was reviled, he did not revile in return' (1 Peter 2:23) — is the model of refusing to return false accusation with false accusation. The neighbor protected from false witness finds its highest expression in Christ's refusal to fabricate charges even against those who fabricated charges against him.
29: Do not say, I will do to him what he did to me — the prohibition against personal revenge. Paul cites the divine prerogative: never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God, for it is written, vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord (Romans 12:19). Jesus extends this to the positive: love your enemies, do good to those who hate you (Luke 6:27). The renunciation of revenge is not passive resignation but the active trust that God's justice is sufficient — and Christ's cross is the definitive act in which God dealt with sin without human revenge being needed.
30: I passed by the field of a lazy man and the vineyard of a man who lacked sense — the wisdom observation that begins the extended sluggard passage. The observational mode here — the wisdom teacher drawing a lesson from a concrete scene — anticipates Jesus's parabolic method: drawing eternal lessons from vineyards, fields, and everyday sights. The field of the sluggard that the teacher passes by is the same genre as the vineyard parables of Jesus (Mark 12:1-12; Matthew 21:33-46), where neglected responsibility has consequences.
31: It was all overgrown with thorns; the ground was covered with nettles, and the stone wall was in ruins — the landscape of neglect. The ruined vineyard of Isaiah 5:1-7 is the prophetic escalation of this image: the beloved's vineyard that yielded wild grapes, interpreted as Israel's failure to produce justice. Jesus applies the vineyard parable to the leaders who failed to tend God's vineyard and will have it taken from them (Matthew 21:43). The overgrown field of Proverbs 24:31 is the seed from which the prophetic vineyard parables of judgment grow.
32: I saw it and took it to heart; I looked and received instruction — wisdom's habit of learning from what is observed. The capacity to receive instruction from the created world is part of the wisdom tradition: 'Go to the ant, you sluggard; consider her ways, and be wise' (Proverbs 6:6). Jesus teaches in parables drawn from birds of the air, lilies of the field, sowers, and vineyards precisely because creation is legible to those who have eyes to see (Matthew 13:13-17). The wisdom habit of learning from what is seen is the posture of the disciple who perceives the kingdom in what Jesus shows.
33: A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest — the incremental self-deception of the lazy person. The same verse appears in Proverbs 6:10; its repetition underlines its diagnostic importance. The NT's urgency: 'Besides this you know the time, that the hour has come for you to wake from sleep. For salvation is nearer to us now than when we first believed' (Romans 13:11). The folding of the hands in the face of eschatological urgency is precisely the failure that the NT warns against; Proverbs provides the wisdom diagnosis, and Paul provides the eschatological motivation to rise.
34: Then poverty will come on you like a wanderer, and your need like an armed man — the sudden arrival of the consequences of sloth. The armed intruder of poverty arriving suddenly mirrors the eschatological warnings: 'The Son of Man is coming at an hour you do not expect' (Matthew 24:44); 'the day of the Lord will come like a thief in the night' (1 Thessalonians 5:2). The sudden arrival of poverty for the sluggard is the wisdom anticipation of the sudden arrival of eschatological accountability. The call to faithful labor — watching, working, waiting — is the NT's application of Proverbs' sloth-warning to the disciples who await the Master's return.
